docs.title: DTWiz 101: The Kubernetes Observability Spellbook
docs.text:
In which a mildly over-caffeinated wizard teaches you to conjure a Kubernetes cluster, summon a Todo app, and make Dynatrace see all - then breaks everything on purpose so you can fix it. Standard wizard behaviour.

Welcome, apprentice. You are about to learn the ancient art of observability the hands-on way: no slides, no hand-waving, just a real cluster you build yourself and a real app you watch Dynatrace watch.

/// caption DTWiz: 100% confidence, 60% documentation. ///

You will learn to Spell used Install Kubernetes from scratch (nothing is pre-baked) conjureCluster Deploy a Todo app onto your cluster deployTodoWizardApp Make Dynatrace observe the app end to end summonDynatrace Diagnose and fix a deliberately broken service dispelChaos

Why this lab is different

Most labs hand you a running cluster. Not here. You will install Kubernetes yourself in Section 01 - because a wizard who cannot conjure their own cluster is just a person in a pointy hat.

  • Begin: Getting Started

docs.title: 00. Getting Started
docs.text: Every wizard needs a workshop. Yours is a Dev Container (in the Dynatrace Enablement App or a GitHub Codespace) that already has kubectl, k3d, helm, k9s, and the framework spells loaded — but, crucially, no running cluster yet. That is on purpose. You will conjure the cluster in Section 01.

docs.title: Your workshop surfaces
docs.text:
URL What it is http://localhost:8000 This training guide (MkDocs) http://localhost:8080 The Todo app web UI (after Section 02) http://localhost:3000 Alternate app port

The Terminal tab is your wand. Everything in this lab is typed there — or run for you by clicking a check button.

docs.title: Warm-up incantation
docs.text:
Confirm the framework spells are loaded. Run the sample helper — it should do arithmetic no wizard should be proud of:

source .devcontainer/util/source_framework.sh
customFunction

No cluster yet - that is expected

If you run kubectl get nodes right now you will get an error. Good. That is the whole point of Section 01.

  • Section 01: Install Kubernetes

docs.title: 01. Install Kubernetes (Conjure a Cluster)
docs.text: No cluster exists yet. A real wizard makes their own. You will install a lightweight Kubernetes distribution (k3d - Kubernetes in Docker) right here in your workshop.

docs.title: Cast the spell
docs.text:
In the Terminal tab, run:

source .devcontainer/util/source_framework.sh
conjureCluster

conjureCluster starts a k3d cluster, wires up the ingress controller, and installs k9s (a delightful terminal UI for Kubernetes). Give it ~30 seconds.

Then admire your handiwork:

kubectl get nodes

You should see one node with status Ready.

k9s: the crystal ball

Run k9s in the Terminal to watch pods live. Press : then type pods, and 0 to see all namespaces. Quit with :q.

docs.title: Verify the cluster is Ready
docs.text: • Section 02: Deploy the Todo App

docs.title: 02. Deploy the Todo App (Summon the Quest Board)
docs.text: Every wizard keeps a quest board. Ours is a humble Todo app - a Node.js API with a web UI, backed by a database, running on the cluster you just conjured. It is deliberately unremarkable, which makes it perfect for watching Dynatrace work.

docs.title: Deploy it
docs.text:
source .devcontainer/util/source_framework.sh
deployTodoWizardApp

This applies the Todo app manifests to your cluster and registers it behind the ingress. When it is ready, open the app UI at http://localhost:8080 and add a quest or two (Defeat the flaky test, Rename a variable, the usual).

docs.title: Generate a little traffic
docs.text:
So Dynatrace has something to observe later, cast a tagged Todo via the API:

generateWizardTraffic

docs.title: Verify the app is Running
docs.text: • Section 03: Observe with Dynatrace

docs.title: 03. Observe with Dynatrace (The All-Seeing Eye)
docs.text: Your app is running, but you are flying blind. Time to summon the all-seeing eye: the Dynatrace Operator deploys OneAgent, which auto-instruments the Todo app - traces, metrics, logs, and topology, with zero code changes.

docs.title: Summon Dynatrace
docs.text:
source .devcontainer/util/source_framework.sh
summonDynatrace

summonDynatrace deploys the Dynatrace Operator, applies an applicationMonitoring DynaKube, and restarts the Todo app so OneAgent can inject into it. This takes a couple of minutes - the eye blinks slowly.

docs.title: Verify OneAgent injected into the Todo app
See it in your tenant (DQL)
docs.text:
Once traffic has flowed for a couple of minutes, confirm Dynatrace is collecting logs from the app. Run this in Notebooks, or click the check:

fetch logs
| filter k8s.namespace.name == "todoapp"
| filter timestamp > now() - 10m
| limit 5
  • Section 04: Troubleshoot and Recommend

docs.title: 04. Troubleshoot and Recommend (Break It On Purpose)
docs.text: A wizard who has never broken production is not a wizard - just lucky. In this final section you will cast a Chaos Spell that takes the Todo app down, watch Dynatrace catch it, then dispel the chaos and confirm recovery. This is the observe -> diagnose -> remediate loop, wizard edition.

docs.title: Cast the Chaos Spell (break it)
docs.text:
source .devcontainer/util/source_framework.sh
castChaosSpell

This scales the Todo app to 0 replicas. Refresh http://localhost:8080 - it is gone. Now open Dynatrace:

  • Kubernetes app -> the todoapp workload shows 0 running pods.
  • Services / Problems -> the service goes unavailable; Davis may raise a problem.

Confirm the outage from the Terminal too:

docs.title: Diagnose
docs.text:
In Dynatrace, the story is obvious once you know where to look. Query recent problems for the namespace:

fetch events
| filter event.kind == "DAVIS_PROBLEM"
| filter not(dt.davis.is_duplicate)
| filter event.status == "ACTIVE"
| filter timestamp > now() - 2h
| fields timestamp, event.name, event.category, event.status
| limit 20

Wizard's recommendation

Availability incidents like this are exactly what Davis anomaly detection and Kubernetes workload alerting are for. In a real environment you would wire a Dynatrace workflow to notify on the problem and, where safe, auto-remediate. For today, you are the workflow.

docs.title: Dispel the Chaos (fix it)
docs.text:
dispelChaos

This scales the Todo app back to 1 replica and waits for it to become healthy. Refresh http://localhost:8080 - the quest board returns, and Dynatrace shows the service recovering.

You are now a DTWiz

You conjured a cluster, summoned an app, opened the all-seeing eye, and survived your own chaos. Go forth and observe responsibly.

  • Resources
  • Cleanup

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Stop the training environment from the platform that started it.

For local development, stop the Dev Container or any local port-forwarding sessions you started during the lab.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
Add topic-specific resources here after generation.

For generated AI trainings, prefer resources that are present in or directly supported by the provided source Markdown.

docs.title: DTWiz 101 - Image assets
docs.text:
The *.png files in this folder are placeholders (currently a reused framework image) so the MkDocs site and the Enablement App render without broken images. Replace each with a funny, on-theme illustration before publishing.

File Intended illustration Used on dtwiz-wizard.png DTWiz the wizard mascot, confident and slightly chaotic index.md dtwiz-conjure.png Wizard conjuring a Kubernetes cluster (k8s wheel) out of thin air 01-install-kubernetes.md dtwiz-todo.png An over-serious "enterprise" Todo app / quest board 02-deploy-todo-app.md dtwiz-eye.png The all-seeing Dynatrace eye watching the app 03-observe-with-dynatrace.md dtwiz-chaos.png Wizard casting a chaos spell and immediately regretting it 04-troubleshoot-and-recommend.md

Guidance: - Keep them light and humorous but professional (this ships in the Dynatrace app). - Square-ish, ~600x600, transparent PNG works best with the align=right layout. - Source from an approved image library or generate; do not use unlicensed art.
